summary: Dan Shapiro follows up on a previous butcher block woodworking project, reporting that the tung oil finish worked well and the block survived heavy use during a large dinner party. He provides five practical recommendations for anyone making their own butcher block, covering wood selection, end-grain construction, finishing techniques, sizing for planers, and safety. The post reflects his hands-on maker enthusiasm, presenting the project as an accessible and rewarding woodworking endeavor that makes great gifts.
key_points: Non-porous hardwoods like hard maple or white oak are best for butcher blocks; avoid toxic spalted maple
End-grain construction is significantly better for heavy cleaver use, confirmed through hands-on experimentation
Finish selection depends on wood porosity: salad bowl finish for open-pore woods, mineral oil for closed-pore
Sizing the block to fit your planer simplifies leveling, and oversized blocks can be cut into multiple gifts
The project is fast, fun, and can be built entirely from workshop scraps
money_quotes: Don't use spalted maple; the fungus that makes the pretty spalting is toxic.
My experimentation confirmed the conventional wisdom that endgrain is much better for projects involving extended use of a meat cleaver.
Don't drink while working with a meat cleaver.
This project is a no-brainer. It's fast, fun, useful, and you can build it entirely with scraps from past projects.
Remember that you can make it 12.5″ x reallyfrigginlong and cut it into multiple butcher blocks when you're done–they make great gifts.
key_insight: Building an end-grain butcher block is a rewarding, accessible woodworking project that yields a durable, functional kitchen tool when you choose the right wood and finish.
